Light precipitation started to arrive on the evening of February 14th due to an approaching low pressure system, with more substantial rain spreading across the area overnight into the 15th as a warm front approached from the south. This promoted a brief instance of thunderstorms on the morning of February 15th, with damaging winds knocking down trees and power lines to parts of the state. While shower coverage diminished from southeast Ohio and northwest West Virginia during the afternoon, moderate to heavy rain continued to trail across northeast Kentucky, southern West Virginia, and southwest Virginia. This precipitation band lifted north with a front during the night, then the passing low pushed additional showers through with a cold front on the 16th. Rain turned to snow as cold air moved in behind the front, then light snow showers lingered into the 17th.||Three to five inches of rain fell across portions of southeast West Virginia. Two to three inches fell in southwest West Virginia. North of I-64, amounts ranged from one to two inches. ||Widespread flooding led to many road closures across West Virginia. However, the worst impacts occurred in southeastern West Virginia where several communities experienced considerable to catastrophic damage. A flash flood emergency was issued for the community of Welch in McDowell County, WV, where flooding invaded multiple homes and businesses, stranded vehicles, and made roads impassable. A vehicle entered the floodwaters and was subsequently swept into the Tug Fork River, with only two of the five occupants surviving.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was observed on Parkview Drive and Parkview Way.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An evolving disturbance churning over the Arklatex region on the evening of February 18th began an eastward trajectory into Central Appalachia that set forth over a 24 hour period of snow across West Virginia. Snow showers first entered into the southwestern coalfields of the state just before the midnight hour of February 19th and steered eastward throughout the early morning. Embedded waves of moisture propelled periods of heavier showers throughout the day on the 19th, before slowly diminishing in the evening for a brief interlude of dry weather. This would then be replaced by renewed snow showers that brought one last burst of accumulating snowfall during the early morning hours of February 20th.  The event wrapped up shortly after daybreak as the low pressure system scooted off the eastern seaboard.||Overall, most of the snow bands remained confined primarily south of the I-64 corridor, where an average of 3 to 5 inches fell in southern West Virginia. The highest snow amounts were measured by the Department of Highways in Northwest Fayette County, averaging between 7 to 8 inches for elevations above 2,600 feet.

On the morning of January 19th, showers spread northeastward through West Virginia, with some locations experiencing a period of rain before lowering surface temperatures prompted a transition to all snow. Precipitation began light, but then became moderate to heavy as a band of snow developed over southeast Ohio, northeast Kentucky, and western West Virginia. This band progressed east across the area during the day, reaching the mountains late in the morning and then exiting by mid afternoon. Behind this band, snow showers continued across the area through the remainder of the day. Snow tapered off from west to east overnight, leaving only light upslope showers over the mountains. This lingering precipitation also came to an end by the afternoon of the 20th. ||Snow amounts ranged from 2 to 4 inches in counties bordering the Ohio River, while the bulk of the lowlands received 4 to 6 inches. The highest snowfall amounts primarily accrued east of I-79 and north of I-64. Most counties in this area reported between 6 and 10 inches of snow. Randolph County reported an impressive 12 to 16 inches of snow. The bulk of snow related impacts occurred while the heavy snow band moved through. In Raleigh County, a multi-vehicle accident occurred on I-77 North near Prosperity. This accident involved several tractor trailers and shut down the interstate for a period of time.

An advancing low pressure system set forth ample amounts of lift and moisture to round out the month of January. Widespread rainfall first entered into the southwestern coalfields of West Virginia on the morning of January 31st, and continued to invade northeastward and up into the higher terrain of the state. Local creeks and streams quickly spilled out of their banks on that day in conjunction with high water issues sprouting within low lying spots as culverts backed up. The southern half of the state encountered the bulk of the flooding impacts from this system, where over an inch and a half of rain fell. Several mainstem rivers also saw a response to the inundation of water, including the Mud River, the Clear Fork River, and the Tygart Valley River. Despite the rain coming to an end on the evening of January 31st, these rivers remained out of their banks into the start of February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Beak Fork came out its banks and flooded portions of Bear Fork Road in Griffithsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of January 19th, showers spread northeastward through West Virginia, with some locations experiencing a period of rain before lowering surface temperatures prompted a transition to all snow. Precipitation began light, but then became moderate to heavy as a band of snow developed over southeast Ohio, northeast Kentucky, and western West Virginia. This band progressed east across the area during the day, reaching the mountains late in the morning and then exiting by mid afternoon. Behind this band, snow showers continued across the area through the remainder of the day. Snow tapered off from west to east overnight, leaving only light upslope showers over the mountains. This lingering precipitation also came to an end by the afternoon of the 20th. ||Snow amounts ranged from 2 to 4 inches in counties bordering the Ohio River, while the bulk of the lowlands received 4 to 6 inches. The highest snowfall amounts primarily accrued east of I-79 and north of I-64. Most counties in this area reported between 6 and 10 inches of snow. Randolph County reported an impressive 12 to 16 inches of snow. The bulk of snow related impacts occurred while the heavy snow band moved through. In Raleigh County, a multi-vehicle accident occurred on I-77 North near Prosperity. This accident involved several tractor trailers and shut down the interstate for a period of time.

An advancing low pressure system set forth ample amounts of lift and moisture to round out the month of January. Widespread rainfall first entered into the southwestern coalfields of West Virginia on the morning of January 31st, and continued to invade northeastward and up into the higher terrain of the state. Local creeks and streams quickly spilled out of their banks on that day in conjunction with high water issues sprouting within low lying spots as culverts backed up. The southern half of the state encountered the bulk of the flooding impacts from this system, where over an inch and a half of rain fell. Several mainstem rivers also saw a response to the inundation of water, including the Mud River, the Clear Fork River, and the Tygart Valley River. Despite the rain coming to an end on the evening of January 31st, these rivers remained out of their banks into the start of February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bengall Hollow Road in the Kistler township was washed out due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system spread snow across the Central Appalachians from the southwest to northeast during the late morning into the evening of January 10th. Snow then continued across the region as the low migrated east during the night. After the departure of the low, moisture associated with an upper trough allowed snow showers to continue through the morning of the 11th. Precipitation coverage eventually tapered off that afternoon into the evening.||By the end of the event, much of the West Virginia lowlands had received two to four inches of snow, although a few counties saw up to five inches in some locations. Amounts ranged from six to ten inches in the higher elevations of the northeast mountains. Outside of this specific area, the mountains generally received four to six inches of snow. Impacts from this event included transportation delays and scattered vehicle accidents across the state.

An evolving disturbance churning over the Arklatex region on the evening of February 18th began an eastward trajectory into Central Appalachia that set forth over a 24 hour period of snow across West Virginia. Snow showers first entered into the southwestern coalfields of the state just before the midnight hour of February 19th and steered eastward throughout the early morning. Embedded waves of moisture propelled periods of heavier showers throughout the day on the 19th, before slowly diminishing in the evening for a brief interlude of dry weather. This would then be replaced by renewed snow showers that brought one last burst of accumulating snowfall during the early morning hours of February 20th.  The event wrapped up shortly after daybreak as the low pressure system scooted off the eastern seaboard.||Overall, most of the snow bands remained confined primarily south of the I-64 corridor, where an average of 3 to 5 inches fell in southern West Virginia. The highest snow amounts were measured by the Department of Highways in Northwest Fayette County, averaging between 7 to 8 inches for elevations above 2,600 feet.

Light precipitation started to arrive on the evening of February 14th due to an approaching low pressure system, with more substantial rain spreading across the area overnight into the 15th as a warm front approached from the south. This promoted a brief instance of thunderstorms on the morning of February 15th, with damaging winds knocking down trees and power lines to parts of the state. While shower coverage diminished from southeast Ohio and northwest West Virginia during the afternoon, moderate to heavy rain continued to trail across northeast Kentucky, southern West Virginia, and southwest Virginia. This precipitation band lifted north with a front during the night, then the passing low pushed additional showers through with a cold front on the 16th. Rain turned to snow as cold air moved in behind the front, then light snow showers lingered into the 17th.||Three to five inches of rain fell across portions of southeast West Virginia. Two to three inches fell in southwest West Virginia. North of I-64, amounts ranged from one to two inches. ||Widespread flooding led to many road closures across West Virginia. However, the worst impacts occurred in southeastern West Virginia where several communities experienced considerable to catastrophic damage. A flash flood emergency was issued for the community of Welch in McDowell County, WV, where flooding invaded multiple homes and businesses, stranded vehicles, and made roads impassable. A vehicle entered the floodwaters and was subsequently swept into the Tug Fork River, with only two of the five occupants surviving.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Coal River at Tornado exceeded its flood stage of 25 feet the morning of February 16, then continued to rise to a crest around 28.2 feet that evening. The river receded back below flood stage the morning of the 17th. Flooding occurred on Smith Creek Road, Ferrell Road, and sections of Coal River Road upstream from Tornado bridge. Backwater along Crooked Creek flooded Ballard Road. Little Coal River Road and Childress Road also flooded.

Local creeks and streams quickly spilled out of their banks on that day in conjunction with high water issues sprouting within low lying spots as culverts backed up. The southern half of the state encountered the bulk of the flooding impacts from this system, where over an inch and a half of rain fell. Several mainstem rivers also saw a response to the inundation of water, including the Mud River, the Clear Fork River, and the Tygart Valley River. Despite the rain coming to an end on the evening of January 31st, these rivers remained out of their banks into the start of February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Craigsville, Cherry Run exceeded out its banks and spilled onto local roads in town.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coon Branch Road was closed due to flooding in Jesse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light precipitation started to arrive on the evening of February 14th due to an approaching low pressure system, with more substantial rain spreading across the area overnight into the 15th as a warm front approached from the south. This promoted a brief instance of thunderstorms on the morning of February 15th, with damaging winds knocking down trees and power lines to parts of the state. While shower coverage diminished from southeast Ohio and northwest West Virginia during the afternoon, moderate to heavy rain continued to trail across northeast Kentucky, southern West Virginia, and southwest Virginia. This precipitation band lifted north with a front during the night, then the passing low pushed additional showers through with a cold front on the 16th. Rain turned to snow as cold air moved in behind the front, then light snow showers lingered into the 17th.||Three to five inches of rain fell across portions of southeast West Virginia. Two to three inches fell in southwest West Virginia. North of I-64, amounts ranged from one to two inches. ||Widespread flooding led to many road closures across West Virginia. However, the worst impacts occurred in southeastern West Virginia where several communities experienced considerable to catastrophic damage. A flash flood emergency was issued for the community of Welch in McDowell County, WV, where flooding invaded multiple homes and businesses, stranded vehicles, and made roads impassable. A vehicle entered the floodwaters and was subsequently swept into the Tug Fork River, with only two of the five occupants surviving.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was observed along Pond Fork Creek and Route 85 in Bim.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of January 19th, showers spread northeastward through West Virginia, with some locations experiencing a period of rain before lowering surface temperatures prompted a transition to all snow. Precipitation began light, but then became moderate to heavy as a band of snow developed over southeast Ohio, northeast Kentucky, and western West Virginia. This band progressed east across the area during the day, reaching the mountains late in the morning and then exiting by mid afternoon. Behind this band, snow showers continued across the area through the remainder of the day. Snow tapered off from west to east overnight, leaving only light upslope showers over the mountains. This lingering precipitation also came to an end by the afternoon of the 20th. ||Snow amounts ranged from 2 to 4 inches in counties bordering the Ohio River, while the bulk of the lowlands received 4 to 6 inches. The highest snowfall amounts primarily accrued east of I-79 and north of I-64. Most counties in this area reported between 6 and 10 inches of snow. Randolph County reported an impressive 12 to 16 inches of snow. The bulk of snow related impacts occurred while the heavy snow band moved through. In Raleigh County, a multi-vehicle accident occurred on I-77 North near Prosperity. This accident involved several tractor trailers and shut down the interstate for a period of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An evolving disturbance churning over the Arklatex region on the evening of February 18th began an eastward trajectory into Central Appalachia that set forth over a 24 hour period of snow across West Virginia. Snow showers first entered into the southwestern coalfields of the state just before the midnight hour of February 19th and steered eastward throughout the early morning. Embedded waves of moisture propelled periods of heavier showers throughout the day on the 19th, before slowly diminishing in the evening for a brief interlude of dry weather. This would then be replaced by renewed snow showers that brought one last burst of accumulating snowfall during the early morning hours of February 20th.  The event wrapped up shortly after daybreak as the low pressure system scooted off the eastern seaboard.||Overall, most of the snow bands remained confined primarily south of the I-64 corridor, where an average of 3 to 5 inches fell in southern West Virginia. The highest snow amounts were measured by the Department of Highways in Northwest Fayette County, averaging between 7 to 8 inches for elevations above 2,600 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system spread snow across the Central Appalachians from the southwest to northeast during the late morning into the evening of January 10th. Snow then continued across the region as the low migrated east during the night. After the departure of the low, moisture associated with an upper trough allowed snow showers to continue through the morning of the 11th. Precipitation coverage eventually tapered off that afternoon into the evening.||By the end of the event, much of the West Virginia lowlands had received two to four inches of snow, although a few counties saw up to five inches in some locations. Amounts ranged from six to ten inches in the higher elevations of the northeast mountains. Outside of this specific area, the mountains generally received four to six inches of snow. Impacts from this event included transportation delays and scattered vehicle accidents across the state.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers first entered into West Virginia on the evening of February 5th as a disturbance skirted by to the south. Precipitation continued into the next day, in addition to a few severe thunderstorms that developed ahead of a cold front in the early afternoon. Strong wind gusts from storms caused numerous downed trees and power line damage across Boone and Lincoln Counties during the early afternoon of February 6th. Another wave of precipitation then passed through the state on the evening and overnight hours of the 6th before coming to an end. ||Throughout the event, a swath of two to four inches of rain fell across portions of central and southern West Virginia. One to two inches fell in northern West Virginia, while locations to the north of US 50 generally received less than an inch of rain.||Flooding began in the state shortly after midnight on the 6th and prevailed throughout the day in the midst of the periods of heavier rainfall. Excess runoff led to rises along many creeks, streams, and smaller rivers. Impacts included some flooded vehicles and structures, mud and rock slides, water rescues, and numerous flooded roads. While high pressure led to drier conditions, minor flooding lingered along parts of the Mud and Tygart Valley Rivers well into the day of February 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tygart Valley River at Mill Creek exceeded a bankfull level of 11 feet the morning of February 6th. The river continued to rise to a crest near 14.1 feet that afternoon, then receded below bankfull the following morning. One half to one foot of water went over Bradley Road at Right Fork of Mill Creek. On Channell Road, several homes were surrounded by water and one water rescue occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light precipitation started to arrive on the evening of February 14th due to an approaching low pressure system, with more substantial rain spreading across the area overnight into the 15th as a warm front approached from the south. This promoted a brief instance of thunderstorms on the morning of February 15th, with damaging winds knocking down trees and power lines to parts of the state. While shower coverage diminished from southeast Ohio and northwest West Virginia during the afternoon, moderate to heavy rain continued to trail across northeast Kentucky, southern West Virginia, and southwest Virginia. This precipitation band lifted north with a front during the night, then the passing low pushed additional showers through with a cold front on the 16th. Rain turned to snow as cold air moved in behind the front, then light snow showers lingered into the 17th.||Three to five inches of rain fell across portions of southeast West Virginia. Two to three inches fell in southwest West Virginia. North of I-64, amounts ranged from one to two inches. ||Widespread flooding led to many road closures across West Virginia. However, the worst impacts occurred in southeastern West Virginia where several communities experienced considerable to catastrophic damage. A flash flood emergency was issued for the community of Welch in McDowell County, WV, where flooding invaded multiple homes and businesses, stranded vehicles, and made roads impassable. A vehicle entered the floodwaters and was subsequently swept into the Tug Fork River, with only two of the five occupants surviving.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Guyandotte River at Pineville exceeded its flood stage of 13 feet the night of February 15, then rose to a crest around 14.1 feet overnight. The river returned below flood stage the next morning. Impacts include flooding of some homes and businesses along Rockcastle Creek near Pine Avenue and Main Street. Flooding of some homes near the confluence of Williams Branch Creek and the Guyandotte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of January 19th, showers spread northeastward through West Virginia, with some locations experiencing a period of rain before lowering surface temperatures prompted a transition to all snow. Precipitation began light, but then became moderate to heavy as a band of snow developed over southeast Ohio, northeast Kentucky, and western West Virginia. This band progressed east across the area during the day, reaching the mountains late in the morning and then exiting by mid afternoon. Behind this band, snow showers continued across the area through the remainder of the day. Snow tapered off from west to east overnight, leaving only light upslope showers over the mountains. This lingering precipitation also came to an end by the afternoon of the 20th. ||Snow amounts ranged from 2 to 4 inches in counties bordering the Ohio River, while the bulk of the lowlands received 4 to 6 inches. The highest snowfall amounts primarily accrued east of I-79 and north of I-64. Most counties in this area reported between 6 and 10 inches of snow. Randolph County reported an impressive 12 to 16 inches of snow. The bulk of snow related impacts occurred while the heavy snow band moved through. In Raleigh County, a multi-vehicle accident occurred on I-77 North near Prosperity. This accident involved several tractor trailers and shut down the interstate for a period of time.

An advancing low pressure system set forth ample amounts of lift and moisture to round out the month of January. Widespread rainfall first entered into the southwestern coalfields of West Virginia on the morning of January 31st, and continued to invade northeastward and up into the higher terrain of the state. Local creeks and streams quickly spilled out of their banks on that day in conjunction with high water issues sprouting within low lying spots as culverts backed up. The southern half of the state encountered the bulk of the flooding impacts from this system, where over an inch and a half of rain fell. Several mainstem rivers also saw a response to the inundation of water, including the Mud River, the Clear Fork River, and the Tygart Valley River. Despite the rain coming to an end on the evening of January 31st, these rivers remained out of their banks into the start of February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Ardel, flooding was observed near the intersection of Cox Road and Route 152.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system spread snow across the Central Appalachians from the southwest to northeast during the late morning into the evening of January 10th. Snow then continued across the region as the low migrated east during the night. After the departure of the low, moisture associated with an upper trough allowed snow showers to continue through the morning of the 11th. Precipitation coverage eventually tapered off that afternoon into the evening.||By the end of the event, much of the West Virginia lowlands had received two to four inches of snow, although a few counties saw up to five inches in some locations. Amounts ranged from six to ten inches in the higher elevations of the northeast mountains. Outside of this specific area, the mountains generally received four to six inches of snow. Impacts from this event included transportation delays and scattered vehicle accidents across the state.

A clipper system tracked from the Ohio Valley into the Central Appalachians Thursday into Friday. The associated upper level disturbance enhanced lift across the area, resulting in snow beginning to fall on the morning of January 3rd and continuing through the day.|Bands of brief heavy snow brought localized higher amounts of snow, and northwest winds behind the passage of the clipper system resulted in upslope flow and additional snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Quinwood, five inches of snow was reported during the evening of January 3rd. Key Impacts: traffic accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An evolving disturbance churning over the Arklatex region on the evening of February 18th began an eastward trajectory into Central Appalachia that set forth over a 24 hour period of snow across West Virginia. Snow showers first entered into the southwestern coalfields of the state just before the midnight hour of February 19th and steered eastward throughout the early morning. Embedded waves of moisture propelled periods of heavier showers throughout the day on the 19th, before slowly diminishing in the evening for a brief interlude of dry weather. This would then be replaced by renewed snow showers that brought one last burst of accumulating snowfall during the early morning hours of February 20th.  The event wrapped up shortly after daybreak as the low pressure system scooted off the eastern seaboard.||Overall, most of the snow bands remained confined primarily south of the I-64 corridor, where an average of 3 to 5 inches fell in southern West Virginia. The highest snow amounts were measured by the Department of Highways in Northwest Fayette County, averaging between 7 to 8 inches for elevations above 2,600 feet.

The highest snowfall amounts primarily accrued east of I-79 and north of I-64. Most counties in this area reported between 6 and 10 inches of snow. Randolph County reported an impressive 12 to 16 inches of snow. The bulk of snow related impacts occurred while the heavy snow band moved through. In Raleigh County, a multi-vehicle accident occurred on I-77 North near Prosperity. This accident involved several tractor trailers and shut down the interstate for a period of time.

On the morning of January 19th, showers spread northeastward through West Virginia, with some locations experiencing a period of rain before lowering surface temperatures prompted a transition to all snow. Precipitation began light, but then became moderate to heavy as a band of snow developed over southeast Ohio, northeast Kentucky, and western West Virginia. This band progressed east across the area during the day, reaching the mountains late in the morning and then exiting by mid afternoon. Behind this band, snow showers continued across the area through the remainder of the day. Snow tapered off from west to east overnight, leaving only light upslope showers over the mountains. This lingering precipitation also came to an end by the afternoon of the 20th. ||Snow amounts ranged from 2 to 4 inches in counties bordering the Ohio River, while the bulk of the lowlands received 4 to 6 inches. An area of low pressure moving northeast across the eastern Ohio Valley produced widespread precipitation across southeast West Virginia beginning Wednesday evening into early Thursday morning. While temperatures for much of southeast West Virginia were just warm enough for most of this precipitation to fall as rain, temperatures across the valleys of Western Greenbrier County where just below freezing, which resulted in icing from freezing rain, which accumulated to around one-quarter of an inch on elevated surfaces.  Rainfall totals were between 2 and 3 inches for the Greenbrier River Valley, some of which fell over partially frozen ground, resulting in increased runoff that produced minor flooding of the Greenbrier River.  Rainfall rates during the event were generally below 0.5 per hour, but occasionally were higher.  CREST Unit Streamflows during this event did not provide returns that would indicate any flooding was taking place.  However, given the partially frozen ground, Hydrophobic Unit Flow was more representative of the flooding situation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gage at Anthony Creek near Anthony (ANYW2) crested at 14.43 feet (5,830 cfs) at 5:20 pm EST on 2/6/25. This is less than a 2-year event (50% AEP) per USGS Streamstats for this location. The Anthony Creek basin received between 2 and 2.75 inches of rainfall in about a 12-hour period ending 10:00 AM EST on 2/6/25, falling over ground that was moistened by widespread rainfall at the beginning of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers first entered into West Virginia on the evening of February 5th as a disturbance skirted by to the south. Precipitation continued into the next day, in addition to a few severe thunderstorms that developed ahead of a cold front in the early afternoon. Strong wind gusts from storms caused numerous downed trees and power line damage across Boone and Lincoln Counties during the early afternoon of February 6th. Another wave of precipitation then passed through the state on the evening and overnight hours of the 6th before coming to an end. ||Throughout the event, a swath of two to four inches of rain fell across portions of central and southern West Virginia. One to two inches fell in northern West Virginia, while locations to the north of US 50 generally received less than an inch of rain.||Flooding began in the state shortly after midnight on the 6th and prevailed throughout the day in the midst of the periods of heavier rainfall. Excess runoff led to rises along many creeks, streams, and smaller rivers. Impacts included some flooded vehicles and structures, mud and rock slides, water rescues, and numerous flooded roads. While high pressure led to drier conditions, minor flooding lingered along parts of the Mud and Tygart Valley Rivers well into the day of February 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Little Kanawha River at Glenville exceeded its flood stage of 23 feet on the afternoon of February 6th. The river crested near 25 feet that evening and then returned below flood stage the following afternoon. While the river was out of its banks, portions of Elm Street flooded, as well as portions of Brooklyn Drive and River Street. At one point, water reached the foundation of the hotel on Conrad Court next to the 1885 bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strongly forced band of convection developed over West Virginia during the morning of March 16th ahead of an approaching front, and quickly lifted north-northeastward into Pennsylvania through the early afternoon. Despite relatively modest instability, very strong deep layer shear, along with largely unidirectional southerly flow and elongated hodographs, supported the continuation of significant severe storms. Large impact was felt from straight-line wind, with gusts in the 60 to 70 MPH range, locally higher, causing widespread damage to trees, power lines, and structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on a power line, blocking the road at 235 F Road in Arthurdale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient in the wake of a cold front caused very strong, gusty winds throughout Southwest Virginia, with winds exceeding 50 MPH at times. The extent of damage was exacerbated by a strong freezing rain event in the previous couple days, both softening soil and weakening tree branches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was blown down by non-thunderstorm winds on Airport Road near the Mercer County Airport, and a couple more in the town of Matoaka.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers first entered into West Virginia on the evening of February 5th as a disturbance skirted by to the south. Precipitation continued into the next day, in addition to a few severe thunderstorms that developed ahead of a cold front in the early afternoon. Strong wind gusts from storms caused numerous downed trees and power line damage across Boone and Lincoln Counties during the early afternoon of February 6th. Another wave of precipitation then passed through the state on the evening and overnight hours of the 6th before coming to an end. ||Throughout the event, a swath of two to four inches of rain fell across portions of central and southern West Virginia. One to two inches fell in northern West Virginia, while locations to the north of US 50 generally received less than an inch of rain.||Flooding began in the state shortly after midnight on the 6th and prevailed throughout the day in the midst of the periods of heavier rainfall. Excess runoff led to rises along many creeks, streams, and smaller rivers. Impacts included some flooded vehicles and structures, mud and rock slides, water rescues, and numerous flooded roads. While high pressure led to drier conditions, minor flooding lingered along parts of the Mud and Tygart Valley Rivers well into the day of February 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Armour Creek rose out of its banks, impacting homes and moving an outbuilding off its foundation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strongly forced band of convection developed over West Virginia during the morning of March 16th ahead of an approaching front, and quickly lifted north-northeastward into Pennsylvania through the early afternoon. Despite relatively modest instability, very strong deep layer shear, along with largely unidirectional southerly flow and elongated hodographs, supported the continuation of significant severe storms. Large impact was felt from straight-line wind, with gusts in the 60 to 70 MPH range, locally higher, causing widespread damage to trees, power lines, and structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the area, multiple trees were reported to be down, along with fence and siding damage.  A lot of airborne debris was also seen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure transitioned from southwest Pennsylvania into upstate New York over the course of the day. . Behind the associated cold front, strong cold advection and a tight pressure gradient resulted in blizzard conditions in the ridges of southwest Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 6 to 8 inches of snow accumulation from 2 PM Sunday through 7 AM Monday was observed with wind gusts exceeding 35 MPH during the entire period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An evolving disturbance churning over the Arklatex region on the evening of February 18th began an eastward trajectory into Central Appalachia that set forth over a 24 hour period of snow across West Virginia. Snow showers first entered into the southwestern coalfields of the state just before the midnight hour of February 19th and steered eastward throughout the early morning. Embedded waves of moisture propelled periods of heavier showers throughout the day on the 19th, before slowly diminishing in the evening for a brief interlude of dry weather. This would then be replaced by renewed snow showers that brought one last burst of accumulating snowfall during the early morning hours of February 20th.  The event wrapped up shortly after daybreak as the low pressure system scooted off the eastern seaboard.||Overall, most of the snow bands remained confined primarily south of the I-64 corridor, where an average of 3 to 5 inches fell in southern West Virginia. The highest snow amounts were measured by the Department of Highways in Northwest Fayette County, averaging between 7 to 8 inches for elevations above 2,600 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system brought widespread rainfall to the area on the 31st of January, initiating flooding in poor drainage areas and along local creeks and streams. While rainfall ended the evening of the 31st, high water and river flooding continued into early February. River flooding persisted along the Little Kanawha River through the 1st of February, while the Tygart Valley and Buckhannon Rivers did not recede below flood stage until the morning of the 2nd.||Water rescues occurred in Belington and Junior in Barbour County, Buckhannon in Upshur County, and near Sullivan in Raleigh County. Lingering high water continued to impact several roads, and a rockslide affected one road in Raleigh County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Knapp Creek at Huntersville exceeded a bankfull level of 9 feet on the night of January 31st. The creek crested around 9.2 feet a few minutes after midnight and then receded below bankfull again during the early morning hours of February 1st. Farmland, fields, and outbuildings adjacent to Knapp Creek and Browns Creek observed flooding while the creek was out of its banks.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Middle Fork Tygart Valley River at Audra exceeded a bankfull level of 11 on feet the morning of January 31st. The river crept up to 11.11 feet just after midnight on February 1st, then receded below bankfull just before sunrise. Impacts observed in the midst of the river spilling out of its banks was some minor flooding of County Route 39 near the bridge.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Elk Twomile Creek at Charleston exceeded a bankfull level of 6.5 feet before daybreak on February 6th. The creek continued to rise to a crest of 8.3 feet and then returned below bankfull later the same morning. The Kanawha-Charleston Humane Association observed water reaching their overflow kennel space while the creek was out of its banks. Flooding also occurred at the intersection of Rutledge Road and Greenbrier Street in downtown Charleston, with water approaching several homes. Rutledge Road was impassable due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light precipitation started to arrive on the evening of February 14th due to an approaching low pressure system, with more substantial rain spreading across the area overnight into the 15th as a warm front approached from the south. This promoted a brief instance of thunderstorms on the morning of February 15th, with damaging winds knocking down trees and power lines to parts of the state. While shower coverage diminished from southeast Ohio and northwest West Virginia during the afternoon, moderate to heavy rain continued to trail across northeast Kentucky, southern West Virginia, and southwest Virginia. This precipitation band lifted north with a front during the night, then the passing low pushed additional showers through with a cold front on the 16th. Rain turned to snow as cold air moved in behind the front, then light snow showers lingered into the 17th.||Three to five inches of rain fell across portions of southeast West Virginia. Two to three inches fell in southwest West Virginia. North of I-64, amounts ranged from one to two inches. ||Widespread flooding led to many road closures across West Virginia. However, the worst impacts occurred in southeastern West Virginia where several communities experienced considerable to catastrophic damage. A flash flood emergency was issued for the community of Welch in McDowell County, WV, where flooding invaded multiple homes and businesses, stranded vehicles, and made roads impassable. A vehicle entered the floodwaters and was subsequently swept into the Tug Fork River, with only two of the five occupants surviving.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tug Fork Fork River at Litwar exceeded its flood stage of 24 feet the night of February 15, then rose to a crest near 31 feet overnight. The river returned below flood stage the morning of the 16th. Major flooding impacted homes along the river between Iaeger and Panther, County Route 1, and several secondary roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high pressure wedge was situated over the region as a surface low pressure system developed over the southern Plains, with an expansive area of precipitation that shifted eastward into the Mid Atlantic during the day of February 11. Precipitation fell as snow through the 11th and into February 12 over southeast West Virginia, and brought significant snowfall amounts, especially in the Greenbrier River Valley. There were some localized power outages due to snow accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 10 and 14 inches of snow fell around Western Greenbrier County. Key Impacts: power outages, downed trees, road closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high pressure wedge was situated over the region as a surface low pressure system developed over the southern Plains, with an expansive area of precipitation that shifted eastward into the Mid Atlantic during the day of February 11. Precipitation fell as snow through the 11th and into February 12 over southeast West Virginia, and brought significant snowfall amounts, especially in the Greenbrier River Valley. There were some localized power outages due to snow accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 4 and 10.5 inches of snow fell around Monroe County, with the highest amounts around Glace and Pickaway. Key Impacts: road closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers first entered into West Virginia on the evening of February 5th as a disturbance skirted by to the south. Precipitation continued into the next day, in addition to a few severe thunderstorms that developed ahead of a cold front in the early afternoon. Strong wind gusts from storms caused numerous downed trees and power line damage across Boone and Lincoln Counties during the early afternoon of February 6th. Another wave of precipitation then passed through the state on the evening and overnight hours of the 6th before coming to an end. ||Throughout the event, a swath of two to four inches of rain fell across portions of central and southern West Virginia. One to two inches fell in northern West Virginia, while locations to the north of US 50 generally received less than an inch of rain.||Flooding began in the state shortly after midnight on the 6th and prevailed throughout the day in the midst of the periods of heavier rainfall. Excess runoff led to rises along many creeks, streams, and smaller rivers. Impacts included some flooded vehicles and structures, mud and rock slides, water rescues, and numerous flooded roads. While high pressure led to drier conditions, minor flooding lingered along parts of the Mud and Tygart Valley Rivers well into the day of February 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Garfield Road closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light precipitation started to arrive on the evening of February 14th due to an approaching low pressure system, with more substantial rain spreading across the area overnight into the 15th as a warm front approached from the south. This promoted a brief instance of thunderstorms on the morning of February 15th, with damaging winds knocking down trees and power lines to parts of the state. While shower coverage diminished from southeast Ohio and northwest West Virginia during the afternoon, moderate to heavy rain continued to trail across northeast Kentucky, southern West Virginia, and southwest Virginia. This precipitation band lifted north with a front during the night, then the passing low pushed additional showers through with a cold front on the 16th. Rain turned to snow as cold air moved in behind the front, then light snow showers lingered into the 17th.||Three to five inches of rain fell across portions of southeast West Virginia. Two to three inches fell in southwest West Virginia. North of I-64, amounts ranged from one to two inches. ||Widespread flooding led to many road closures across West Virginia. However, the worst impacts occurred in southeastern West Virginia where several communities experienced considerable to catastrophic damage. A flash flood emergency was issued for the community of Welch in McDowell County, WV, where flooding invaded multiple homes and businesses, stranded vehicles, and made roads impassable. A vehicle entered the floodwaters and was subsequently swept into the Tug Fork River, with only two of the five occupants surviving.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple sections of US 152, in the Moses Fork area, were covered by water. Jennies Creek exceeded its banks and flooded the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strongly forced band of convection developed over West Virginia during the morning of March 16th ahead of an approaching front, and quickly lifted north-northeastward into Pennsylvania through the early afternoon. Despite relatively modest instability, very strong deep layer shear, along with largely unidirectional southerly flow and elongated hodographs, supported the continuation of significant severe storms. Large impact was felt from straight-line wind, with gusts in the 60 to 70 MPH range, locally higher, causing widespread damage to trees, power lines, and structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported to have fallen on to a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Occluding surface low pressure tracked across the Upper Ohio Valley and into western New York by the morning of January 1st. Rain transitioned to snow behind a crossing cold front in strong cold advection. Upslope enhancement led to significant snow accumulation in eastern Tucker County, with less reported in Preston County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm total of 6.4 inches of snow was reported at both Davis and Canaan Heights.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system brought widespread rainfall to the area on the 31st of January, initiating flooding in poor drainage areas and along local creeks and streams. While rainfall ended the evening of the 31st, high water and river flooding continued into early February. River flooding persisted along the Little Kanawha River through the 1st of February, while the Tygart Valley and Buckhannon Rivers did not recede below flood stage until the morning of the 2nd.||Water rescues occurred in Belington and Junior in Barbour County, Buckhannon in Upshur County, and near Sullivan in Raleigh County. Lingering high water continued to impact several roads, and a rockslide affected one road in Raleigh County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tygart Valley River at Belington exceeded its flood stage of 14 feet on the night of January 31st. After cresting around 16.3 feet the next morning, the river returned below flood stage the morning on February 2nd. In downtown Belington, the Willow Street bridge and the intersection of Talbott and Wabash Roads flooded while the river was out of its banks. Around 8 am, a water rescue occurred at the intersection of Talbott and Wabash Roads in Belington. Between 11 am and Noon, another water rescue occurred in Junior near the intersection of West River Avenue and Fox Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tygart Valley River at Philippi exceeded its flood stage of 19 feet beginning on the night of January 31st. After cresting around 21.4 feet early the next morning, the river returned below flood stage on the evening of February 1st. While the river was out of its banks, flooding occurred on Chestnut Street, McCartney Street, Sycamore Street and Beech Avenue in downtown Philippi. The Vietnam Veterans Park and the intersection of US 119 and Truck Route 250 also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Behind a potent cold front, strong gradient winds developed across much of the region during the late morning to afternoon hours on February 16.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Eastern West Virginia Regional Airport in Martinsburg, WV, KMRB, measured a peak wind gust of 62 mph at 1545 LST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front interacted with an unstable airmass due to unusually warm temperatures during the late morning hours of the 16th. This sparked scattered thunderstorms across southeast West Virginia, with one storm in Greenbrier County generating winds that brought down three trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down three trees near the intersection of Big Draft Road and Anthony Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of a cold front, strong northwesterly flow and very cold temperatures created a favorable environment for localized high levels of snowfall in the mountains of southeastern WV.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven inches of snow were observed in Quinwood, West Virginia. Key Impacts - Localized road closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strongly forced band of convection developed over West Virginia during the morning of March 16th ahead of an approaching front, and quickly lifted north-northeastward into Pennsylvania through the early afternoon. Despite relatively modest instability, very strong deep layer shear, along with largely unidirectional southerly flow and elongated hodographs, supported the continuation of significant severe storms. Large impact was felt from straight-line wind, with gusts in the 60 to 70 MPH range, locally higher, causing widespread damage to trees, power lines, and structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down, blocking one lane of a road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system brought widespread rainfall to the area on the 31st of January, initiating flooding in poor drainage areas and along local creeks and streams. While rainfall ended the evening of the 31st, high water and river flooding continued into early February. River flooding persisted along the Little Kanawha River through the 1st of February, while the Tygart Valley and Buckhannon Rivers did not recede below flood stage until the morning of the 2nd.||Water rescues occurred in Belington and Junior in Barbour County, Buckhannon in Upshur County, and near Sullivan in Raleigh County. Lingering high water continued to impact several roads, and a rockslide affected one road in Raleigh County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A water rescue occurred on Sullivan Road at McVey Cemetery Road, Dublin Street, and Woodford Street in Sullivan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure coming out of the Gulf moved over the Carolinas and Virginia, bringing substantial moisture along with it. An arctic front descended from the Great Lakes and through the Ohio Valley, interacting with the moisture before it traveled off the Atlantic Coast, forming snow showers around the Mid-Atlantic.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy snow showers created localized accumulations of up to 5 inches in the Bluefield area. Key Impacts: Poor travel conditions due to snow covered roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient in the wake of a cold front caused very strong, gusty winds throughout Southwest Virginia, with winds exceeding 50 MPH at times. The extent of damage was exacerbated by a strong freezing rain event in the previous couple days, both softening soil and weakening tree branches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moving northeast across the eastern Ohio Valley produced widespread precipitation across southeast West Virginia beginning Wednesday evening into early Thursday morning. While temperatures for much of southeast West Virginia were just warm enough for most of this precipitation to fall as rain, temperatures across the valleys of Western Greenbrier County where just below freezing, which resulted in icing from freezing rain, which accumulated to around one-quarter of an inch on elevated surfaces.  Rainfall totals were between 2 and 3 inches for the Greenbrier River Valley, some of which fell over partially frozen ground, resulting in increased runoff that produced minor flooding of the Greenbrier River.  Rainfall rates during the event were generally below 0.5 per hour, but occasionally were higher.  CREST Unit Streamflows during this event did not provide returns that would indicate any flooding was taking place.  However, given the partially frozen ground, Hydrophobic Unit Flow was more representative of the flooding situation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain accumulated to around one-quarter of an inch, mainly on elevated surfaces, near Renick.  Key Impacts: power outages and icy bridges and overpasses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strongly forced band of convection developed over West Virginia during the morning of March 16th ahead of an approaching front, and quickly lifted north-northeastward into Pennsylvania through the early afternoon. Despite relatively modest instability, very strong deep layer shear, along with largely unidirectional southerly flow and elongated hodographs, supported the continuation of significant severe storms. Large impact was felt from straight-line wind, with gusts in the 60 to 70 MPH range, locally higher, causing widespread damage to trees, power lines, and structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down, blocking a road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down in the road along with power lines at the intersection of Long Hollow Road and Pebble Rock Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moving northeast across the eastern Ohio Valley produced widespread precipitation across southeast West Virginia beginning Wednesday evening into early Thursday morning. While temperatures for much of southeast West Virginia were just warm enough for most of this precipitation to fall as rain, temperatures across the valleys of Western Greenbrier County where just below freezing, which resulted in icing from freezing rain, which accumulated to around one-quarter of an inch on elevated surfaces.  Rainfall totals were between 2 and 3 inches for the Greenbrier River Valley, some of which fell over partially frozen ground, resulting in increased runoff that produced minor flooding of the Greenbrier River.  Rainfall rates during the event were generally below 0.5 per hour, but occasionally were higher.  CREST Unit Streamflows during this event did not provide returns that would indicate any flooding was taking place.  However, given the partially frozen ground, Hydrophobic Unit Flow was more representative of the flooding situation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Raders Valley Road was inundated around 4 inches deep due to runoff from prolonged rainfall and partially frozen ground.  No damage was reported to the road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gage at the Greenbrier River at Ronceverte (RONW2) crested at 16.11 feet at 5:20 am EST on 2/7/25. This is the highest crest on record since March 2021. The Greenbrier basin received between 2 and 3 inches of rainfall in about a 12-hour period ending 10:00 AM EST on 2/7/25, falling over ground that was moistened by widespread rainfall at the beginning of the month.  High water spilling out of the river caused River Road to be flooded and impassible just downstream from the gage.  Further downstream, Fort Spring Pike was also reported impassible by flooding from the river.  No damage was reported to either roadway.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gage at the Greenbrier River at Alderson, WV (ALDW2) crested at 15.33 feet (42,200 cfs) at 8:15 am EST on 2/7/25. This is roughly a 2-year event (50% AEP) per USGS Streamstats for this location and the highest crest on record since 2014. The Greenbrier basin received between 2 and 3 inches of rainfall in about a 12-hour period ending 10:00 AM EST on 2/7/25, falling over ground that was moistened by widespread rainfall at the beginning of the month. This event is between a 2-year and a 5-year Average Recurrence Interval (50%-20% Annual Exceedance Probability) per USGS StreamStats.  Several roads from south of Alderson to Talcott to near Hilldale were reported inundated and impassible by flooding of the Greenbrier River, including portions of Highway 3/12 as well as Howell Street in Alderson, during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light precipitation started to arrive on the evening of February 14th due to an approaching low pressure system, with more substantial rain spreading across the area overnight into the 15th as a warm front approached from the south. This promoted a brief instance of thunderstorms on the morning of February 15th, with damaging winds knocking down trees and power lines to parts of the state. While shower coverage diminished from southeast Ohio and northwest West Virginia during the afternoon, moderate to heavy rain continued to trail across northeast Kentucky, southern West Virginia, and southwest Virginia. This precipitation band lifted north with a front during the night, then the passing low pushed additional showers through with a cold front on the 16th. Rain turned to snow as cold air moved in behind the front, then light snow showers lingered into the 17th.||Three to five inches of rain fell across portions of southeast West Virginia. Two to three inches fell in southwest West Virginia. North of I-64, amounts ranged from one to two inches. ||Widespread flooding led to many road closures across West Virginia. However, the worst impacts occurred in southeastern West Virginia where several communities experienced considerable to catastrophic damage. A flash flood emergency was issued for the community of Welch in McDowell County, WV, where flooding invaded multiple homes and businesses, stranded vehicles, and made roads impassable. A vehicle entered the floodwaters and was subsequently swept into the Tug Fork River, with only two of the five occupants surviving.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major flash flooding occurred in and around Welch. Homes and businesses were surrounded by water, roads and underpasses became impassable, and cars were stranded. People became trapped in their vehicles on Riverside Drive before the Havaco bridge. In Kimball, water entered multiple homes, businesses, and cars. A vehicle with five occupants drove into floodwaters and was swept into the Tug Fork River. Two people were able to make it to safety while the other three occupants perished.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed over western Maryland and eastern West Virginia, then significantly gathered strength as it moved east into the I-95 corridor. Destructive winds, very large hail, and a tornado were observed with some of the most intense storms. Additional thunderstorms in a weakening but still severe state crossed the Appalachians overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 52 knots (60 MPH) was measured by the AWOS at Greater Cumberland Regional Airport (KCBE).

While rainfall ended the evening of the 31st, high water and river flooding continued into early February. Lingering high water continued to impact several roads, and a rockslide affected one road in Raleigh County.

The river crested around 10.7 feet just after midnight on February 1st and then receded below bankfull during the late morning hours. While out of its banks, flooding occurred near homes along the river downstream of the gage.

Air temperatures fell into the single digits if not negative single digits for several nights.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water covered Mate Hatfield Lane and Bulldog Boulevard near Delbarton, the Left Fork of Bens Creek Road off King Coal Highway, and Parsley Bottom near Lenore. Multiple areas of Rutherford Hollow flooded. Water rose to the headlights of a lifted truck near Mullins Tire. Residences on State Route 65 in Red Jacket also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strongly forced band of convection developed over West Virginia during the morning of March 16th ahead of an approaching front, and quickly lifted north-northeastward into Pennsylvania through the early afternoon. Despite relatively modest instability, very strong deep layer shear, along with largely unidirectional southerly flow and elongated hodographs, supported the continuation of significant severe storms. Large impact was felt from straight-line wind, with gusts in the 60 to 70 MPH range, locally higher, causing widespread damage to trees, power lines, and structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported to have fallen on a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two consecutive days of thunderstorms producing primarily large hail swept through West Virginia on May 4th and 5th. Hail stones ranged from quarter sized and up to two inches in diameter in some cases. Many trained spotters observed hail covering the ground in places such as Buckhannon and Elkview on the 4th, then becoming more common across Kanawha and Wood Counties on the 5th. This was primarily due to low freezing levels at the time of the storms, allowing growing storms to capitalize on easier hail production. Storms also produced instances of damaging wind gusts, which resulted in downed trees and power lines. Thunderstorm activity on both days were heavily dictated by daytime heating, causing storm coverage and intensity to diminish quickly after sunset.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The state of West Virginia was placed into the warm sector of an advancing low pressure system on May 30th in the wake of a lifting warm front. This allowed for strong daytime heating and high moisture levels to act together to create strong thunderstorms during the afternoon. A cold front eventually swept into the area that evening to bring the risk for severe weather to an end, but not before downed trees and power lines were observed in the state as a result of the strong thunderstorm activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A downed tree blocked Brushy Fork Road in the Atlas township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north through West Virginia on May 15th, followed by a cold front approaching from the west and stalling across central Ohio on the 16th. A low pressure system began to pivot northeast out of the Great Lakes region and eventually pushed the cold front eastward across the area on the 17th.||Severe thunderstorms that developed during the afternoon and evening of the 16th produced locally damaging wind gusts that downed trees and caused power outages across portions of West Virginia, northeast Kentucky, southeast Ohio, and southwest Virginia. Another round of rain and severe storms passed through in the early morning hours of the 17th, with precipitation exiting the central Appalachians around daybreak.||Rainfall amounts generally remained around an inch or less in southeast Ohio and northern West Virginia, while 1 to 2.5 inches fell across portions of northeast Kentucky, southwest Virginia, and southern West Virginia. The highest rainfall amounts, approaching 3.5 inches, were observed across portions of McDowell County in West Virginia and Buchanan County in Virginia. Excessive rainfall prompted localized flash flooding in McDowell County on May 17th before storms came to an end and floodwaters could recede.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A downed tree was observed near Missouri 3 Mile Road.

Another round of rain and severe storms passed through in the early morning hours of the 17th, with precipitation exiting the central Appalachians around daybreak.||Rainfall amounts generally remained around an inch or less in southeast Ohio and northern West Virginia, while 1 to 2.5 inches fell across portions of northeast Kentucky, southwest Virginia, and southern West Virginia. The highest rainfall amounts, approaching 3.5 inches, were observed across portions of McDowell County in West Virginia and Buchanan County in Virginia. Excessive rainfall prompted localized flash flooding in McDowell County on May 17th before storms came to an end and floodwaters could recede.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water was observed on Panther Mohawk Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two consecutive days of thunderstorms producing primarily large hail swept through West Virginia on May 4th and 5th. Hail stones ranged from quarter sized and up to two inches in diameter in some cases. Many trained spotters observed hail covering the ground in places such as Buckhannon and Elkview on the 4th, then becoming more common across Kanawha and Wood Counties on the 5th. This was primarily due to low freezing levels at the time of the storms, allowing growing storms to capitalize on easier hail production. Storms also produced instances of damaging wind gusts, which resulted in downed trees and power lines. Thunderstorm activity on both days were heavily dictated by daytime heating, causing storm coverage and intensity to diminish quickly after sunset.

The state of West Virginia was placed into the warm sector of an advancing low pressure system on May 30th in the wake of a lifting warm front. This allowed for strong daytime heating and high moisture levels to act together to create strong thunderstorms during the afternoon. A cold front eventually swept into the area that evening to bring the risk for severe weather to an end, but not before downed trees and power lines were observed in the state as a result of the strong thunderstorm activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree and power line damage resulted in 480 customers to be without power around West Union.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary stalled over the state of West Virginia beginning on May 11th, then dissolved over the area while a disturbance approached from the southwest on the 12th. Widespread precipitation progressed northward across the area on the 12th, followed by rounds of scattered showers and thunderstorms continuing to impact the area over the next couple of days as an upper level trough and surface low pressure system traversed the Ohio Valley. ||During the afternoon and evening of May 13th, strong thunderstorms downed trees and power lines across portions of the area. Heavy rainfall within storms also led to widespread half to one and a half in rainfall amounts, with pockets of 2 to 4 inch rainfall amounts across portions of West Virginia. Heavy rain over previously saturated ground prompted flash flooding in Braxton, Clay, Harrison, Jackson, and Lewis Counties. Mudslides also occurred in Boone and Lewis Counties, with one instance of a mobile home being evacuated due to water reaching chest level.||The risk for severe weather concluded by May 14th once the surface low sprinted off the eastern seaboard. Floodwaters also receded in the wake of showers and storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was observed in the Little Birch and Crites Mountain area.

A frontal boundary stalled over the state of West Virginia beginning on May 11th, then dissolved over the area while a disturbance approached from the southwest on the 12th. Widespread precipitation progressed northward across the area on the 12th, followed by rounds of scattered showers and thunderstorms continuing to impact the area over the next couple of days as an upper level trough and surface low pressure system traversed the Ohio Valley. ||During the afternoon and evening of May 13th, strong thunderstorms downed trees and power lines across portions of the area. Heavy rainfall within storms also led to widespread half to one and a half in rainfall amounts, with pockets of 2 to 4 inch rainfall amounts across portions of West Virginia. Heavy rain over previously saturated ground prompted flash flooding in Braxton, Clay, Harrison, Jackson, and Lewis Counties. Mudslides also occurred in Boone and Lewis Counties, with one instance of a mobile home being evacuated due to water reaching chest level.||The risk for severe weather concluded by May 14th once the surface low sprinted off the eastern seaboard. Floodwaters also receded in the wake of showers and storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was observed on Crawford Road from Laurel Run and Redlick Run Road from Redlick Run. Smith Run exceeded its banks, entered homes along Smith Run Road, and flooded parts of Lewis County Park. Multiple roads were closed in Weston. Cars became stranded in water along Johnathon Run Road. One trailer home was evacuated due to chest deep water. Mudslides were also observed in the Walkersville area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two consecutive days of thunderstorms producing primarily large hail swept through West Virginia on May 4th and 5th. Hail stones ranged from quarter sized and up to two inches in diameter in some cases. Many trained spotters observed hail covering the ground in places such as Buckhannon and Elkview on the 4th, then becoming more common across Kanawha and Wood Counties on the 5th. This was primarily due to low freezing levels at the time of the storms, allowing growing storms to capitalize on easier hail production. Storms also produced instances of damaging wind gusts, which resulted in downed trees and power lines. Thunderstorm activity on both days were heavily dictated by daytime heating, causing storm coverage and intensity to diminish quickly after sunset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local power outages occurred in the Walton area due to downed trees and power lines along Rocky Branch Road in Walton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted into the vicinity of West Virginia on the night of May 29th and remained present overhead until being ushered east by a low the evening of the 30th. Behind this system, a cold front approached from the north and slowly descended southward through the area on the 31st.||Rounds of rain and storms moved across the area on the 30th, with several severe thunderstorms downing power lines and numerous trees during the afternoon. While the majority of precipitation tapered off by the morning of the 31st, a few additional showers sprouted up in parts of southeastern West Virginia during the afternoon. ||The bulk of the area received less than an inch of rain, though some parts of northern, southwest and southeast West Virginia accrued up to around 2 inches. Soils had become increasingly saturated during subsequent rounds of rain and storms earlier in the month; thus, even somewhat lesser rainfall amounts were enough to cause flooding across portions of Doddridge, Harrison, Mason, Tyler, and Wood Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was observed at Route 23 and Broad Run in the town of Ashley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system brought a warm front and then cold front into West Virginia on May  28th. The cold front became stationary and then weakened over the area later that night. While rainfall amounts for the 28th were mainly less than an inch and a half, a couple of inches fell over localized portions of Harrison and Taylor Counties. Rainfall from the previous day had also helped to saturate the ground, making flooding easier to occur. In Harrison and Taylor Counties, several roadways flooded and at least one water rescue occurred in the midst of the heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was observed on and around Good Hope Pike from Sunny Croft Country Club to Davisson Run Road and Milford Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large surface low pressure system slowly moved across the northern Plains, with the associated cold front stalling across the Ohio Valley. A mesoscale convective system developed over Kentucky and tracked east towards the central Appalachians, bringing storms the area by the afternoon of May 16. The cold pool from the advancing MCS provided enough lift for convection to break through the morning inversion, and plenty of daytime heating increased SBCAPE to 4000 J/kg. The mesoscale environment was conducive for large hail and damaging winds, which led to numerous reports of downed trees and hail an inch or larger in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered thunderstorms arrived into West Virginia on the afternoon of May 8th ahead of a cold front. Damaging wind gusts accompanying these storms resulted in downed trees and power lines across several counties in the southwestern portions of the state. In Mingo County, a Mesonet station in the town of Ragland measured a 58 mile per hour gust as strong storms moved through the area. There was also an isolated instance of quarter size hail observed in Logan County. The front slid through late that night and concluded the severe weather threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over 300 customers were without power in the Fort Gay area due to tree and power line damage.

The highest rainfall amounts, approaching 3.5 inches, were observed across portions of McDowell County in West Virginia and Buchanan County in Virginia. Excessive rainfall prompted localized flash flooding in McDowell County on May 17th before storms came to an end and floodwaters could recede.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A downed tree made contact with power lines in the area of Little Sycamore Road and Lizemore Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary stalled over the state of West Virginia beginning on May 11th, then dissolved over the area while a disturbance approached from the southwest on the 12th. Widespread precipitation progressed northward across the area on the 12th, followed by rounds of scattered showers and thunderstorms continuing to impact the area over the next couple of days as an upper level trough and surface low pressure system traversed the Ohio Valley. ||During the afternoon and evening of May 13th, strong thunderstorms downed trees and power lines across portions of the area. Heavy rainfall within storms also led to widespread half to one and a half in rainfall amounts, with pockets of 2 to 4 inch rainfall amounts across portions of West Virginia. Heavy rain over previously saturated ground prompted flash flooding in Braxton, Clay, Harrison, Jackson, and Lewis Counties. Mudslides also occurred in Boone and Lewis Counties, with one instance of a mobile home being evacuated due to water reaching chest level.||The risk for severe weather concluded by May 14th once the surface low sprinted off the eastern seaboard. Floodwaters also receded in the wake of showers and storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large surface low pressure system slowly moved across the northern Plains, with the associated cold front stalling across the Ohio Valley. A mesoscale convective system developed over Kentucky and tracked east towards the central Appalachians, bringing storms the area by the afternoon of May 16. The cold pool from the advancing MCS provided enough lift for convection to break through the morning inversion, and plenty of daytime heating increased SBCAPE to 4000 J/kg. The mesoscale environment was conducive for large hail and damaging winds, which led to numerous reports of downed trees and hail an inch or larger in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed by thunderstorm winds and blocked the road in the 5000 block of Raders Valley Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered thunderstorms arrived into West Virginia on the afternoon of May 8th ahead of a cold front. Damaging wind gusts accompanying these storms resulted in downed trees and power lines across several counties in the southwestern portions of the state. In Mingo County, a Mesonet station in the town of Ragland measured a 58 mile per hour gust as strong storms moved through the area. There was also an isolated instance of quarter size hail observed in Logan County. The front slid through late that night and concluded the severe weather threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down onto Perry Branch Road in Chapmanville and obstructed traffic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two consecutive days of thunderstorms producing primarily large hail swept through West Virginia on May 4th and 5th. Hail stones ranged from quarter sized and up to two inches in diameter in some cases. Many trained spotters observed hail covering the ground in places such as Buckhannon and Elkview on the 4th, then becoming more common across Kanawha and Wood Counties on the 5th. This was primarily due to low freezing levels at the time of the storms, allowing growing storms to capitalize on easier hail production. Storms also produced instances of damaging wind gusts, which resulted in downed trees and power lines. Thunderstorm activity on both days were heavily dictated by daytime heating, causing storm coverage and intensity to diminish quickly after sunset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell onto power lines near the Staunton Turnpike in Parkersburg. Local power outages were observed around the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The state of West Virginia was placed into the warm sector of an advancing low pressure system on May 30th in the wake of a lifting warm front. This allowed for strong daytime heating and high moisture levels to act together to create strong thunderstorms during the afternoon. A cold front eventually swept into the area that evening to bring the risk for severe weather to an end, but not before downed trees and power lines were observed in the state as a result of the strong thunderstorm activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several downed trees were observed along Hookersville Road Bunch in Muddlety.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving closed upper level low originating from the Gulf of America brought anomalous moisture into the area. A long duration period of rain ensued, with rain rates exceeding two inches an hour during the afternoon of May 13th. The slow moving storms coupled with training led to localized rainfall totals of four to eight inches across Mineral County, WV, with lesser amounts of two to four inches across the rest of the eastern panhandle. Most of this rain fell in a 6 hour period, resulting in flash flooding especially across Mineral County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Road closed at 15000 Bloomery Pike due to high water.
